claims.claim: The SEI film begins to decompose at approximately 90°C, exposing the anode and its lithium-embedded carbon components to the electrolyte, which then reacts exothermically with the electrolyte, causing the temperature to further rise.
claims.quote: As the temperature climbs to 90 ◦C, the SEI layer begins to disintegrate, exposing the anode and its lithium-embedded carbon components to the electrolyte, which then reacts exothermically with the electrolyte, causing the temperature to further rise.

claims.claim: When the temperature exceeds 130°C, the diaphragm starts to melt and progressively fractures, causing an internal short circuit through direct contact of the positive and negative electrodes.
claims.quote: When the temperature exceeds 130 ◦C, the diaphragm starts to melt and progressively fractures, and the interior of the battery is shorted by the direct contact of the positive and negative electrodes.

claims.claim: At temperatures up to 200°C, both the cathode and electrolyte start to break down and interact with the adhesive, releasing toxic, dangerous, and combustible gases such as HF, hydrogen, carbon monoxide, and hydrocarbons.
claims.quote: Up to 200 ◦C, both the cathode and electrolyte start to break down and even interact with the adhesive, releasing a significant amount of toxic, dangerous, and combustible gases, such as HF, hydrogen, carbon monoxide, and hydrocarbons.

claims.claim: In NCM and LFP cells, the release of flammable gas occurs approximately 1585 s and 987 s before the fire, respectively, indicating that gas signals provide earlier warning than temperature, voltage, and pressure signals.
claims.quote: Cui et al. [130] discovered that in NCM and LFP cells, the release of flammable gas occurs approximately 1585 s and 987 s before the fire, respectively. The results indicate that the unique gas signal can not only provide an early warning during thermal runaway but also provide an earlier warning than the temperature, voltage, and pressure signals.

claims.claim: Using gas signal as the benchmark, an early warning can be given 7.05 min before thermal runaway peaks, compared to only 5.96 min for voltage signals and 0.5 min for temperature signals.
claims.quote: the figure indicates that when the gas signal is used as the benchmark, an early warning can be given 7.05 min before thermal runaway peaks, giving more time for escape and prevention, as opposed to when the voltage and temperature signals are used, which only allow for 5.96 min and 0.5 min of evacuation time, respectively.

claims.claim: The dynamic impedance in the 30–90 Hz band changes from negative to positive when the battery is overcharged, and taking 70 Hz impedance as an example, its warning time is 580 s earlier than the thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Lyu et al. [129] showed that the dynamic impedance in the 30–90 Hz band changes from negative to positive when the battery is overcharged. Taking 70 Hz impedance as an example, its warning time is 580 s earlier than the thermal runaway, successfully avoiding the thermal runaway accident caused by overcharging.

claims.claim: H2 is the first detected gas among six common gases (H2, CO, CO2, HCl, HF, and SO2) during LFP battery overcharging experiments, with detection time 639 s in advance of smoke and 769 s in advance of fire.
claims.quote: the results of the validation of the overcharging experiments of the LFP battery show that H2 is the first detected among six common gases, namely, H2, CO, CO2, HCl, HF, and SO2, and the detection time is 639 s in advance of the smoke and 769 s in advance of the fire.

claims.claim: The substable components of the SEI film start to break down at approximately 90°C, releasing gases including CO2 and O2 as well as combustible hydrocarbons.
claims.quote: The substable components of the SEI film start to break down at a temperature of approximately 90oC, releasing gases including CO2 and O2 as well as combustible hydrocarbons.

claims.claim: Most semiconductor gas sensors need to operate at temperatures above 200°C in practical applications, which is much higher than the operating temperature range of lithium-ion batteries (−20°C to 60°C), making them difficult to encapsulate in lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: most semiconductor gas sensors need to operate at temperatures above 200 ◦C to 60 ◦C in practical applications, and suffer from high operating temperatures, high power consumption, low stability, poor selectivity and complex synthesis processes. The operating temperature range of lithium-ion batteries is from −20 ◦C to 60 ◦C, which is much lower than the operating temperature of metal-oxide semiconductor sensors, resulting in gas sensors that are difficult to encapsulate in lithium-ion batteries.

claims.claim: The model-based fault diagnosis algorithm for ISC detection successfully triggered the alarm 30 min before thermal runaway occurred in the case of RISC = 0.35 Ω ISC.
claims.quote: In the case of RISC = 0.35 Ω ISC, the algorithm successfully triggered the alarm 30 min before the thermal runaway occurred.

claims.claim: The PVDF binder reacts quickly with lithium metal or LiC6 at 350°C, producing fluoride gases and H2.
claims.quote: Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), a binder, for instance, reacts quickly with lithium metal or LiC6 at 350oC as shown below.

claims.claim: During discharge of a cylindrical 18650 LIB with an embedded FBG sensor, the temperature difference between the center temperature and the tank temperature is as high as 6°C, while during charging the temperature difference is 3°C.
claims.quote: During the discharge process, the temperature difference between the center temperature and the tank temperature is as high as 6 ◦C, while during the charging process, the temperature difference is 3 ◦C.

claims.claim: The surface temperature of a LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2 cell can reach 877°C when subjected to thermal abuse, and for a 3500 mAh cell, the total volume of gases released during deflagration was 5495 cm3.
claims.quote: They discovered that the surface temperature of the cell could reach 877oC when subjected to thermal abuse and that for a 3500 mAh cell, the total volume of gases released during deflagration was 5495 cm3, with the emitted gases including H2, CO2, CH4, CO, C2H4, and C2H6.

claims.claim: Traditional BMS technology can only provide early warning measures for obvious battery failures and cannot realize intelligent processing of data signals and prediction of unknown accidents.
claims.quote: traditional BMS technology can only provide early warning measures for obvious battery failures and cannot realize intelligent processing of data signals and prediction of unknown accidents.

claims.claim: Gas sensors have more early warning capability than sensors based on internal signature monitoring, but both lack predictive capability.
claims.quote: Gas sensors have more early warning capability than sensors based on internal signature monitoring, but both lack predictive capability.

claims.claim: Fire-impingement on LIBs resulted in temperatures in the immediate vicinity ranging between 200 °C and 900 °C, which aggravated thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: fire-impingement resulted in elevated temperatures in the immediate vicinity of the LIBs that were in the range of between 200 °C and 900 °C. Such temperature aggravated thermal runaway (TR) propagation, resulting in rapid temperature rise within the battery module and even explosions after 20 min of 'smoldering period'.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway and subsequent fire and explosion were attributed to the violent reduction of the cathode material coexisting with electrolyte when temperature exceeded 260 °C.
claims.quote: The thermal runaway and subsequent fire and explosion observed in the heating test was attributed to the violent reduction of the cathode material which coexisted with the electrolyte when the temperature exceeded 260 °C.

claims.claim: The self-accelerating decomposition temperature (SADT) calculated using the Semenov model was 126.1 °C and using the Frank-Kamenetskii model was 139.2 °C for the NCM/LTO battery.
claims.quote: The self-accelerating decomposition temperature of the cell, regarded as the critical temperature to trigger TR propagation, was calculated as 126.1 and 139.2 °C using the classical Semenov and Frank-Kamenetskii models.

claims.claim: NCM material in contact with electrolyte showed a sharp increase in heat flow from about 250 °C, reaching a peak of 1162.8 mW/g at 282.8 °C, with pressure increasing rapidly from zero to 3 bar above 270 °C.
claims.quote: a slow rise of the heat flow began from 150 °C and increased sharply from about 250 °C and reached the peak at 282.8 °C. The maximum heat flow was 1162.8 mW g−1. At temperature above 270 °C, following the generation of gases from the thermochemical reactions, the pressure increased rapidly from zero to 3 bar.

claims.claim: If the ambient temperature exceeds 126.1 °C or the shell temperature of the surrounding battery reaches 139.2 °C and the time exceeds the delay time of TR, thermal runaway would propagate from the failure cell to surrounding cells.
claims.quote: if the energy from the burnt battery is sufficient to make the ambient temperature over 126.1 °C or heat the surrounding batteries to 139.2 °C, and the time beyond the delay time of TR, TR would propagate from the failure cell to the surrounding cells although the thermal runaway progress of failure cell finished.

claims.claim: Increasing nickel content in cathode materials causes thermal decomposition to appear at a lower temperature and increases the amount of heat released.
claims.quote: With increasing the nickel content, the thermal decomposition of the cathode appears at a lower temperature, and the amount of heat released increases.

claims.claim: The decomposition of the s-SEI layer is more activated at high temperature than the decomposition of the p-SEI layer.
claims.quote: It has been examined that the decomposition of the s-SEI layer is more activated at a high temperature than the decomposition of the p- SEI layer.

claims.claim: Above 300°C, the binder reaction with lithium-ions is the main exothermic reaction at the anode, accompanied by H2 generation.
claims.quote: Previous studies reported that, above 300 ◦C, the binder reaction with lithium-ions is the main exothermic reaction at the anode with H2 generation.

claims.claim: When the electrolyte is present at the anode, s-SEI regeneration (R13) is activated and results in more exothermic heat flow of s-SEI decomposition (R14) than in the absence of the electrolyte; however, depletion of lithium-ions by R13 reduces the exothermic peak of the binder reaction (R15).
claims.quote: The presence of the electrolyte at the anode activates s-SEI regeneration (R13) and results in more exothermic heat flow of s-SEI decomposition (R14) than in the absence of the electrolyte. In terms of the heat flow in the binder reaction (R15), R15 is less active because R13 depletes available lithium-ions.

claims.claim: The separator reaction (R7) occurs at a lower temperature when oxygen is present: it occurs at 320.09°C under 5% oxygen atmosphere, compared to 442.85°C without the cathode (argon atmosphere), and proceeds more quickly when oxygen content increases from 5% to 10%.
claims.quote: The separator reaction (R7) is generally more effective when oxygen is present in the system. As can be seen in Fig. S5, it can be confirmed that the separator is reacted at 320.09 ◦C under 5% oxygen atmosphere. Additionally, the reaction proceeds more quickly when the oxygen content in the Argon balance increase to 10% as compared to the 5% condition.

claims.claim: At 250°C isothermal condition, the pyrolysis reaction rate of EMC is 5.25×10⁻⁷/s, and it would take more than 7,000,000 s for all EMC to pyrolyze; for EC the rate is 4.94×10⁻¹⁴/s and it would take more than 300,000,000 s to fully pyrolyze, making pyrolysis inconsistent with the thermal runaway time scale of approximately 10 s.
claims.quote: At 250 ◦C, the pyrolysis reaction rates of EC and EMC are 4.94x10-14/s (compared to 2.10 × 10-2/s at 900 K) and 5.25x10-7/s (compared to 6.31x10-2/s at 700 K), respectively... At 250 ◦C isothermal condition, it takes more than 7,000,000 s for all EMC to pyrolyze, and more than 300,000,000 s for EC.

claims.claim: The combination of cathode and anode induces additional H2 and CO2 generation (R16) at around 250°C, attributed to the reaction of the anode with oxygen produced from the structural collapse of the cathode.
claims.quote: the combination of the cathode and the anode induces additional H2 and CO2 generation reaction (R16) at around 250 ◦C, and it is presumed to be the reaction of the anode with oxygen produced from the structural collapse of the cathode.

claims.claim: The rock-salt transformation reaction (R10) of the cathode occurs at a lower peak temperature when electrolyte is present compared to without electrolyte.
claims.quote: the peak temperature of R10 decreases compared to its peak in Fig. 3(a), implying the influence of the electrolyte on R10. This variation is confirmed more clearly using the deconvoluted peak of R10 in Fig. 3(e).

claims.claim: The reaction model achieves RMSE values of 1.91 mW for the electrolyte group, 21.79 mW for the cathode group, and 4.53 mW for the anode group when compared to DSC results.
claims.quote: The reaction model exhibits root-mean-square-error of 1.91 mW, 21.79 mW, and 4.53 mW in the electrolyte, the cathode and the anode, respectively, as compared to differential scanning calorimeter results.
